published_prior_art.cite: BoushehrianBadriAkbariKiyavash2026, Appendix D.1: general shift invariance and a normalization, but not this explicit transversal formulation.
BoushehrianBadriAkbariKiyavash2026, Sections 3.1–3.2: the response-type LP, its dual, and finite vertex evaluation give sharp ATE endpoints.
BoushehrianBadriAkbariKiyavash2026 provides a multivalued vertex family, but not this displayed gauge-fixed (2,3) witness.
BoushehrianBadriAkbariKiyavash2026 proves signature recognition only for the binary-instrument case.
BoushehrianBadriAkbariKiyavash2026 leaves sharp multivalued characterization for future work; AvisFukuda1996 supplies only generic reverse-search architecture.
BoushehrianBadriAkbariKiyavash2026, Theorem 4 already gives the relevant multivalued lower-bound family, but not an exhaustive-count comparison.

novelty_flags.label: Open-ended Question 3 (strict incompleteness)
novelty_flags.one_line: The displayed comparator count uses (l-1)^(n-1), whereas Boushehrian et al.'s nonconstant s in ([l]\{a})^n family has l((l-1)^n-(l-1)) members.

novelty_flags.label: Theorem 2 (dual sharp endpoints)
novelty_flags.one_line: This is the standard finite response-type LP/dual vertex representation already supplied by the cited Boushehrian et al. setup; it is supporting infrastructure, not a new result.

soundness_flags.label: Open-ended Question 3 (strict incompleteness)
soundness_flags.one_line: Exceeding the incorrectly smaller count would not certify incompleteness of the published family; the comparison must use its actual cardinality.

source_verification_receipts.locator: pp. 10–11, Section 4.2, Theorem 4; p. 67, proof counting conclusion
source_verification_receipts.verified_claim: Theorem 4 indexes its multivalued family by a distinguished arm a and a nonconstant vector s in ([l] \ {a})^n, yielding l((l-1)^n-(l-1)) M-distinct vertices.

The paper formulates the finite response-type primal and dual, states that sharp bounds are obtained by finite dual-vertex evaluation, and gives the sharp ATE endpoint representation in the binary-instrument specialization.

The categorical-IV paper gives necessary, sufficient, nonredundant linear inequalities, computes ATE bounds via linear programming, and gives a KL-based convex-program finite-sample inference procedure; it also states that all-arm plug-in intervals are no wider and sometimes strictly tighter.

strengthening_paths.suggestion: Correct Open-ended Question 3 to compare |V_{n,l}| with l((l-1)^n-(l-1)), then require an explicit vertex invariant showing the new witness cannot belong to Boushehrian et al.'s arm-indexed family.

Sharpen the main enumeration question with an explicit basis encoding, parent-map predicate, bit-complexity convention, and a worked (n,l)=(2,3) reverse-search transition from primitive active equations.
Demote Theorem 2 to stated LP background or replace it with a genuinely new multivalued consequence, such as a proved non-family vertex regime or a certified recognition criterion.

recommended_next_step: Revise the erroneous lower-bound comparator count and its incompleteness logic, retain the polynomial-delay enumeration question as the field-tier kernel, then resubmit.
